---
title: "Final"
author: "Soochun An"
date: "2025-07-21"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r}
library(factoextra)
library(mclust)
library(dplyr)
library(magrittr)
library(cluster)

set.seed(10000)
movies <- read.csv("C:/Users/asd05/Desktop/4051/movies.csv")
summary(movies)
str(movies) # Check chr
movies_data <- nrow(movies)
  
na_data <- is.na(movies) #check NA
table(na_data)## NA table
colSums(is.na(movies[, c("Year", "Title", "Rating", "Duration..min.")])) 
#Check for NA in each column
colMeans(is.na(movies)) 
#Check the percentage of NAs in each column, and consider removing columns with a high percentage of missing values.

clean_data <- movies %>% filter(complete.cases(select(., Year, Duration..min., Rating)))
summary(select(clean_data, Duration..min., Year, Rating))
#Summary of the data after removing NA 
after_na_removed <- nrow(clean_data)# check 
print(after_na_removed)
removed_by_Na <- movies_data - after_na_removed
print(removed_by_Na)

boxplot(clean_data$Duration..min., main = "Outlier check")
#Check for outliers using boxplots.
boxplot(clean_data$Year, main = "Outlier check")


remove_outliers_box <- function(x) {
  q1 <- quantile(x, 0.25)
  q3 <- quantile(x, 0.75)
  iqr <- q3 - q1
  lower <- q1 - 1.5 * iqr
  upper <- q3 + 1.5 * iqr
  return(x >= lower & x <= upper)
} ## Outlier removal formula

clean_data <- clean_data %>%
  filter(
    remove_outliers_box(Year),
    remove_outliers_box(Duration..min.),
  ) 
## Apply the outlier removal formula to each variable, but perform it only for Duration and Year since only these variables affect scaling and clustering.
 
boxplot(clean_data$Duration..min., main = "After outlier removed")
boxplot(clean_data$Year, main = "After outlier removed")
summary(select(clean_data, Duration..min., Year, Rating))
after_outlier_removed <- nrow(clean_data)
print(after_outlier_removed)
removed_by_outlier <- after_na_removed - after_outlier_removed 
print(removed_by_outlier)

data_cluster <- scale(clean_data[, c("Year", "Duration..min.")])
##The data is scaled because the variables are on different scales.

fviz_nbclust(data_cluster, kmeans, method = "wss") +
  labs(title = "Elbow Method for Determining Optimal k")
# Elbow plot to determine the optimal number of clusters

km_result <- kmeans(data_cluster, centers = 3, nstart = 50)# set nstart to 50.
fviz_cluster(km_result, data = data_cluster, geom = "point", ellipse.type = "convex") 
  ggtitle("K-means Clustering of Movies")
clean_data$km_result <- as.factor(km_result$cluster)

Kmeans_summary <- clean_data %>%
  group_by(km_result) %>%
  summarise(avg_year = round(mean(Year), 0),
            ## The year does not need to be in decimal form
    avg_duration = mean(Duration..min.), 
    avg_rating = mean(Rating),
    count = n())
print(Kmeans_summary)


top_movies_by_Kmeans <- clean_data %>%
  group_by(km_result) %>%
  arrange(desc(Rating)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 5) %>%
  select(km_result, Title, Rating, Year, Duration..min.)
#To recommend movies by cluster, we extract the top 5 highest-rated movies from each cluster.
print(top_movies_by_Kmeans)


d <- dist(data_cluster, method = "euclidean")
hc_result1  <- hclust(d, method = "complete")
hc_result2 <- hclust(d, method = "single")
hc_result3 <- hclust(d, method = "average")
hc_clusters1 <- cutree(hc_result1, k = 3)
hc_clusters2<- cutree(hc_result2, k = 3)
hc_clusters3<- cutree(hc_result3, k = 3)
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(hc_result1, main = "Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram Complete")
plot(hc_result2, main = "Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram single")
plot(hc_result3, main = "Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram average")

fviz_cluster( list(data = data_cluster, cluster = hc_clusters1),
  geom = "point", ellipse.type = "convex") + ggtitle("Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram Complete")
fviz_cluster( list(data = data_cluster, cluster = hc_clusters2),
  geom = "point", ellipse.type = "convex") + ggtitle("Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram single")
fviz_cluster( list(data = data_cluster, cluster = hc_clusters3),
  geom = "point", ellipse.type = "convex") + ggtitle("Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram average")
clean_data$hc_cluster1 <- as.factor(hc_clusters1)
## Complete linkage was chosen because it produced the most well-separated and evenly distributed clusters. 



hc_summary <- clean_data %>%
  group_by(hc_cluster1) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_year = mean(Year),
    avg_duration = mean(Duration..min.), 
    avg_rating = mean(Rating),
    count = n())
print(hc_summary)


top_movies_by_hc_cluster <- clean_data %>%
  group_by(hc_cluster1) %>%
  arrange(desc(Rating)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 5) %>%
  select(hc_cluster1, Title, Rating, Year, Duration..min.)
print(top_movies_by_hc_cluster)



gmm_result1 <- Mclust(data_cluster)
##Since GMM automatically finds the optimal number of clusters, Just check the optimal number of clusters 
summary(gmm_result1)
fviz_mclust(gmm_result1, what = "classification", geom = "point", main = "GMM Clustering")

gmm_result2 <- Mclust(data_cluster, G = 3)
##To compare with other clustering methods, we fix G (number of clusters), although it may vary 
summary(gmm_result2)
fviz_mclust(gmm_result2, what = "classification", geom = "point", main = "GMM Clustering")
clean_data$gmm_cluster2 <- as.factor(gmm_result2$classification)




GMM_summary <- clean_data %>%
  group_by(gmm_cluster2) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_year = round(mean(Year), 0),
    avg_duration = mean(Duration..min.), 
    avg_rating = mean(Rating),
    count = n())
print(GMM_summary)


top_movies_by_GMM <- clean_data %>%
  group_by(gmm_cluster2) %>%
  arrange(desc(Rating)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 5) %>%
  select(gmm_cluster2, Title, Rating, Year, Duration..min.)
print(top_movies_by_GMM)






```

##Summary  
Mean imputation can distort the data and lead to biased clustering results, especially for distance-based methods like K-means. Since the missing rate was low, removing rows with NA was a safer and more accurate choice.

Outliers were removed using the IQR method because it is not affected by extreme values and can detect outliers without assuming any specific data distribution.
